policy_statement: Use renewable energy (solar, wind, green hydrogen) for seawater desalination in NEOM to meet potable water needs.
mechanism_description: The city will use renewable energy (solar, wind, green hydrogen) to run seawater desalination, reducing the emissions associated with producing potable water.
primary_causal_pathway: Fossil-powered desalination → switch to renewable energy → ↓ electricity/fuel combustion emissions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city changes the energy source used to run seawater desalination plants (and potentially associated pumping/pretreatment/post-treatment) from grid/fossil energy to dedicated renewables (solar/wind) and/or green hydrogen.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels and reduced fossil-derived grid generation for the same kWh/mechanical energy set per m³ of water; emissions reductions depend on the carbon intensity of the displaced electricity/fuels and the lifecycle footprint of renewable generation/hydrogen.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower CO₂e emissions per unit of potable water produced (and lower total sectoral emissions if desalination output is maintained or expanded with low-carbon energy).
dominant_pathway_test: Switching desalination energy supply from fossil-based electricity/fuels to renewables reduces lifecycle GHG emissions from potable water production.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s defining lever is substituting renewable energy for fossil energy in desalination operations, directly lowering GHG emissions from energy use.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While desalination supports water reliability (an adaptation co-benefit), the mechanism as stated targets emissions from producing potable water; the largest climate-relevant effect is emissions reduction, not reduced exposure to a hazard. It is also not primarily “Resource Efficiency” because it does not necessarily reduce energy/water demand—rather it changes the carbon intensity of supply. Not “Nature-Based Solutions” because it relies on engineered energy substitution.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy’s main purpose is drought resilience and emissions impacts are incidental, it could be framed as adaptation at the policy level; however, for the canonical mechanism “renewable-powered desalination → desalination emissions reduction,” the dominant effect across instances is mitigation via decarbonized energy input.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that powers desalination with renewables (or green hydrogen) to reduce desalination-related emissions follows the same mitigation pathway of lowering fossil energy combustion/grid emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy also qualifies for an adaptation co-benefit (water security) or includes additional measures (efficiency, brine management) must be determined from policy-specific evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If renewable power enables major expansion of desalination that increases total electricity demand, net emissions outcomes depend on additionality and the carbon intensity of the displaced grid generation; classification remains Mitigation because the mechanism’s intended primary effect is reducing emissions per unit of desalinated water by decarbonizing the energy supply.
key_indicators: "using renewable energy resources such as solar, wind and green hydrogen"; "seawater desalination"; "potable water needs"
co_benefits: Improved water security/reliability; potential air quality benefits from reduced local fossil combustion
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a procurement requirement (renewable supply contracts) or target-setting, but the statement is phrased as actual deployment/use of renewables for desalination rather than a requirement, standard, or target.
